did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, and VGG19, to perform automatic classification of benign and malignant skin cancers using dermoscopic images. The study involved training these models on 10,000 images from Kaggle datasets using transfer learning to obtain classification accuracy results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the comparative evaluation of seven deep learning architectures (DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, VGG19, etc.) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods including data preprocessing, transfer learning on 10,000 Kaggle images, and two data split schemes (60:20:20 and 80:10:10). The study reports new empirical results, such as test accuracy metrics (e.g., 90.7% for DenseNet169) and inference times, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Evaluation of CNN Architectures for Skin Cancer Classification
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study presents a comparative evaluation of seven deep learning architectures, including DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, and VGG19, for the automatic classification of benign and malignant skin cancers using dermoscopic images. The researchers compiled a dataset of 10,000 images from three public Kaggle sources and applied transfer learning with ImageNet weights to train the models. Two data split schemes (60:20:20 and 80:10:10) were utilized to assess model robustness and generalizability. Experimental results demonstrated that DenseNet169 achieved the highest test accuracy of 90.7%, while MobileNetV2 proved to be the fastest with an inference time of 16 seconds. The findings highlight the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, supporting the use of DenseNet169 for robust classification and MobileNetV2 for real-time applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two main categories used in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 2,942 (Scheme 1) or 3,998 (Scheme 2) benign images before augmentation, totaling 5,884 or 7,976 after augmentation.

One of the two main categories used in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 3,058 (Scheme 1) or 4,012 (Scheme 2) malignant images before augmentation, totaling 6,116 or 8,024 after augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant skin cancer categories
all_task_types.details: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: benign and malignant skin cancer. The study evaluates seven CNN architectures (DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, VGG19, etc.) using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Combined Kaggle Dataset (SCMBD + MSCD + MCID)
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic skin cancer images compiled from three public Kaggle datasets: Skin Cancer Malignant and Benign Dataset (SCMBD), Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset (MSCD), and Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset (MCID). The images were resized to 224x224 pixels and split into benign and malignant classes.

use.training: Used for training seven CNN architectures (DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, VGG19, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, VGG16) using transfer learning with ImageNet weights. Two split schemes were applied: Scheme 1 (60% training, 20% validation, 20% testing) and Scheme 2 (80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing). Data augmentation was applied to the training subsets.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor model performance (accuracy and loss) during training and to apply early stopping. Scheme 1 used 2,000 images; Scheme 2 used 1,000 images.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and inference time). Scheme 1 used 2,000 images; Scheme 2 used 1,000 images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the performance of the classification distribution of true (correct) and false (incorrect) predictions from each class, calculating True Positive (TP), True Negative (TN), False Positive (FP), and False Negative (FN).

To evaluate model performance using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score (including macro and weighted averages) to measure the efficiency and balance of predictions between benign and malignant classes.

To compare the performance of seven CNN architectures (DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, VGG19, etc.) across two data split schemes (60:20:20 and 80:10:10) based on validation accuracy, validation loss, test accuracy, test loss, and inference time.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between CNN architectures were based on descriptive statistics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and inference time.

Scheme 1 (60:20:20 Split).description: Training: 6,000 images (augmented to 12,000). Validation: 2,000 images. Testing: 2,000 images.
Scheme 1 (60:20:20 Split).notes: DenseNet169 achieved the highest validation accuracy (87.07%) and lowest validation loss (29.87%) in this scheme. MobileNetV2 had the fastest inference time (16s).

Scheme 2 (80:10:10 Split).description: Training: 8,000 images (augmented to 16,000). Validation: 1,000 images. Testing: 1,000 images.
Scheme 2 (80:10:10 Split).notes: DenseNet169 achieved the highest test accuracy (90.7%) and validation accuracy (89.57%) across all experiments. MobileNetV2 had the fastest inference time (14s).

architecture_used.main_model: Seven CNN architectures: DenseNet169, MobileNetV2, VGG19, InceptionV3, ResNet50, Xception, and VGG16
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning based on ImageNet weights (initial weights trained on ImageNet dataset)

architecture_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Fine-tuning strategy where some early layers of the pre-trained model were kept frozen, while the final layers were retrained using the skin cancer dataset

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including horizontal flip, rotation (20 degrees), zoom (0.2), shear (0.2), width shift (0.2), height shift (0.2), scaling, translation, and brightness adjustment

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of image transformations
Applied as part of image transformations
Applied as part of image transformations

conclusions: DenseNet169 achieved the highest test accuracy of 90.7% and demonstrated superior robustness and balanced precision-recall performance, making it the most reliable model for skin cancer classification.

MobileNetV2 was the most efficient model with the fastest inference time (14 to 16 seconds) and competitive accuracy (up to 88%), making it suitable for low-resource devices and real-time clinical applications.
VGG19 offered high accuracy (up to 88.4%) and balanced classification metrics, serving as a good choice for systems with high precision requirements and sufficient computing resources.
There is a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency among the evaluated CNN architectures, with DenseNet169 prioritizing accuracy and MobileNetV2 prioritizing speed.
The study is limited by dataset diversity regarding ethnicity, lighting, and geographic distribution.
The study did not incorporate advanced ensemble learning, lesion segmentation, or explainable AI (XAI) techniques.

Future work should focus on improving generalization through multi-source data integration, exploring model interpretability via XAI methods such as Grad-CAM, and developing lightweight web- or mobile-based diagnostic tools.
CNN-based architectures, particularly DenseNet169, have significant potential to contribute to advancing medical image analysis and AI-assisted dermatological diagnostics.
These AI systems are designed to support clinical decision-making and accelerate the diagnostic process rather than replace dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study relies on a combined dataset of 10,000 images from three public Kaggle sources (SCMBD, MSCD, MCID), which limits generalizability due to lack of diversity in ethnicity, lighting, and geographic distribution.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on internal test splits (20% or 10% of the dataset), raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance differences between architectures.
Data augmentation was applied only to the training subset, which may not fully mitigate potential overfitting or bias present in the original Kaggle datasets.
The study did not incorporate explainable AI (XAI) techniques or lesion segmentation, limiting the interpretability and clinical trustworthiness of the predictions.
Inference times reported (e.g., 14-16 seconds for MobileNetV2) are likely measured on standard desktop GPUs and may not reflect performance on actual resource-constrained clinical devices.

Class imbalance in the original subsets (e.g., 2,942 benign vs. 3,058 malignant in Scheme 1) was addressed via balanced batch sampling, but the overall dataset composition may still reflect biases from the source Kaggle datasets.
The models were trained using transfer learning with ImageNet weights, which may not be optimally suited for medical dermoscopic images compared to models pre-trained on medical datasets.
